marketState: neutral — extreme heat demand battles bearish storage surplus and weak seasonal drift
summary: Weather is the dominant near-term driver: demand is above the 5-year maximum for both the 1-7D and 8-16D forecast windows, running +56.5 BCF/d (+46%) above 5-year normal at 180.5 BCF/d and still surging +26.6 in the last 24 hours — an exceptional heat pulse that stands as the single most bullish signal in the dataset. Against it, the structural picture is bearish: seasonality assigns only 19% probability of being higher in 4 weeks (typical drift -1.8%, Low reliability), storage sits +151 Bcf above 5-year average with a +6 Bcf bearish injection surprise, and price at $3.279 is $0.319 above the $2.960 fair-value midpoint though comfortably inside the $2.46-$3.46 band. The crucial contrarian overlay is COT: managed-money net short at the 18th 5-year percentile (-82,722 contracts) represents a crowded-short extreme; when an extreme demand catalyst aligns with a crowded short base, short-squeeze episodes historically produce sharp but short-lived rallies. The fair-value model itself provides a subtle bullish drift — the midpoint rises from $2.960 now to $3.184 in 21 days, meaning the structural gravity is less of a headwind than it first appears.

longTerm.rationale: Seasonality is the primary structural headwind: 19% historical probability of being higher in 4 weeks with a typical -1.8% drift, while storage at +151 Bcf above 5-year average will continue to build through injection season. However, managed-money shorts at the 18th 5-year percentile represent extreme crowding that limits how far prices can fall — a deep seasonal dip is possible but a crash to $2.50 is unlikely until shorts cover. The winter curve (Dec $4.028, Jan $4.432 vs front $3.279) offers a compelling seasonal-spread trade, but entry on dips toward the $3.00-3.10 zone (near the +21D fair-value midpoint of $3.184 and consistent with a 2-3% seasonal decline) would materially improve risk/reward for a winter-targeting long versus entering at current slightly-above-fair-value levels.
longTerm.invalidation: Storage surplus widens to +200 Bcf above 5y average AND weather normalizes to within 5-year range simultaneously — that combination would validate a sustained downtrend toward $2.70-2.80. Alternatively, MM positioning covers back to the 40th+ 5-year percentile (crowded-short tailwind already exhausted).
longTerm.trigger: Enter LONG when price pulls back to $3.00-3.10 zone with 8-16D demand still above or near 5-year maximum; stop below $2.85, TP1 $3.40, TP2 $3.68 (upper band in 21 days) for approximately 1:2.5 R:R

shortTerm.entry: $3.24 area (current level or intraday dip; 5m/15m RSI at 34-34.5 flags near-term oversold on shorter timeframes)
shortTerm.rationale: Both the 1-7D and 8-16D demand forecasts are above the 5-year maximum — a two-week weather extreme that puts immediate squeeze pressure on the 18th-percentile-short managed-money base. The 24-hour demand surge of +26.6 BCF/d confirms the heat event is intensifying, not fading. The 4-hour timeframe shows the only clean bullish trend with positive MACD histogram; the 5m/15m RSIs near 34 indicate a recent intraday pullback has created a tactical entry rather than a blow-off top. Price remains inside the fair-value band (room to $3.46 upper cap) and the fair-value midpoint itself rises modestly over the coming weeks, so structural gravity is limited.
shortTerm.invalidation: Daily close below $3.08 signals the heat premium is not being priced in and structural bears are dominating; alternatively, either the 1-7D or 8-16D demand forecast falls back inside the 5-year range (weather catalyst removed)

keyDrivers: Demand above 5-year maximum on both 1-7D and 8-16D windows: current 180.5 BCF/d is +46% vs 5y normal and surging +26.6 in 24 hours
Managed-money net short at 18th 5-year percentile (-82,722 contracts): historically extreme crowding creates short-squeeze risk against a heat catalyst
Storage surplus +151 Bcf above 5y average with +6 Bcf bearish injection surprise: structural ceiling that limits sustained rallies once weather normalizes
Seasonality only 19% up probability in 4 weeks (typical -1.8% drift) but Low reliability (51 pp spread) — clear directional lean but wide uncertainty
Winter curve premium: Dec $4.028, Jan $4.432 vs front $3.279 — $1.15 seasonal spread signals an eventual long opportunity on any dip to $3.00-3.10
risks: Weather forecast normalizes within 5-7 days: any shift of the 8-16D demand outlook back inside the 5-year range removes the primary bullish catalyst and likely re-prices the front month back toward the $2.96 fair-value midpoint
Storage builds accelerate post-heatwave: if injections run at 80-90 Bcf per week as summer demand tapers, the +151 Bcf surplus could widen toward +200 Bcf and reinforce seasonal selling pressure
